[Page 10 | Introduction | A Note About Peripheral Nerve Supply]
The peripheral nervous system (PNS) comprises all the neural structures outside the brain and spinal cord, which constitute the central nervous system (CNS). The PNS has two main components: the somatic nervous system and the autonomic nervous system (ANS); the latter deals with involuntary control of smooth muscle and glands. As this book is concerned with skeletal muscles, it is only the somatic nervous system that is of interest. The PNS consists of 12 pairs of cranial nerves and 31 pairs of spinal nerves, along with their subsequent branches. The spinal nerves are numbered according to the level of the spinal cord from which they arise, known as the spinal segment. In this book the relevant peripheral nerve supply is listed with each muscle. However, the spinal segment from which the nerve fibers arise often varies between different sources. This is because spinal nerves are organized into networks known as plexuses (plexus = a network of nerves: from Latin plectere = “to braid”), which supply different regions of the body, and nerve fibers from different spinal segments will contribute to the individual named nerve that supplies a particular muscle. For each muscle in this book, the spinal levels that typically contribute to its named nerve are indicated. The relevant spinal segments are represented by C for cervical, T for thoracic, L for lumbar, and S for sacral, followed by a number representing the level. Numbers in parentheses indicate a smaller contribution. Figure: A spinal segment, showing the nerve roots combining to form a spinal nerve, which then divides into ventral and dorsal rami. ** A spinal segment is the part of the spinal cord that gives rise to each pair of spinal nerves, one for each side of the body. Each spinal nerve contains sensory and motor fibers from the dorsal and ventral roots respectively. Soon after the spinal nerve exits through the foramen or opening between adjacent vertebrae, it divides into a dorsal primary ramus, which is directed posteriorly, and a ventral primary ramus, which is directed anteriorly and laterally. Fibers from the dorsal rami innervate the skin and extensor muscles of the neck and trunk.

Trigger points can be active (painful) or inactive (latent); they can also manifest in secondary muscles or as satellites in and around the vicinity of the primary pain. They can mimic angina, bursitis, prostatitis, appendicitis, cystitis, arthritis, esophagitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, pelvic inflammatory disease, diverticulosis, costochondritis, sciatica, and pain from a heart or gall bladder attack. The term trigger point was coined in 1942 by Dr. Janet Travell to describe painful lumps or nodules felt within tight bands of muscle. Trigger points all seem to have the following characteristics: Small nodules the size of a pinhead; Pea-sized nodules; Large lumps; Several large lumps next to each other; Tender spots embedded in taut bands of semi-hard muscle that feels like a cord; Rope-like bands lying next to each other like partially cooked spaghetti; Skin over a trigger point slightly warmer than the surrounding skin (due to increased metabolic/autonomic activity). What is trigger point therapy? Trigger point therapy covers a range of techniques aimed at deactivating these painful knots. Many approaches are practical and “hands-on”; they can be performed at home with a partner or on your own with trigger point “tools.” Combined with some simple lifestyle changes, trigger point therapy can yield dramatic, immediate, and sustainable results. How do I know it is a trigger point? You are looking for: Stiffness in the affected/host muscle; Spot tenderness (exquisite pain); A palpable taut nodule or band; Presence of referred pain; Reproduction of the symptoms (accurate); Possible loss of skin elasticity in the region of the trigger point. The affected area may be moister or warmer (or colder) than the surrounding tissues, and may feel a little like sandpaper. Figure: 1.4. Hot zones. How do I know when I have done enough pressing? Hold the trigger point for 6 seconds: If the pain diminishes rapidly, stay with it until the trigger point softens or evaporates beneath your pressure. If the pain stays the same or gets worse, come away for 15 seconds and then try again. Repeat 3 times if necessary. If the trigger point still does not deactivate after the third repetition, note it down as it may be a secondary or satellite point. What do I do after I have come away from the point? Follow all deep work with a gentle generalized effleurage massage. The area where you did the deep work may still be tender, but do not avoid it. This will help to dispel pain-inducing toxins from the area and stimulate the repair of the fascia. Figure: 1.5. Pain, often exquisite, is present at a discrete point. A nodule is embedded within a taut band in the muscle. Pressure reproduces the pain symptoms, with radiations in a specific and reproducible distribution (map). Pain cannot be explained by findings from a neurological examination. One of the most important features of trigger points is that they may be embedded in the muscles remotely from where the pain is felt. It is partly for this reason that so many therapies fail to help. More often than not, therapists and doctors tend to look at the place that hurts rather than find the source of the pain. A trigger point makes its host muscle shorter and fatter and reduces its efficiency: this can lead to pressure on nerves and blood vessels. Understanding trigger points and their maps will help guide you toward finding the source of your pain.

Self-treatment will help you to understand, manage, and control your pain on your own, at home, and without a therapist. Once you get used to working with trigger points, you may even find your friends, relatives, and neighbors all want treatment. Who knows, you may even become a therapist yourself one day! Throughout this book I have indicated the most effective self-help techniques and stretches based on my many years of practice. What equipment do I need? You should use a bed (or a couch), although sometimes a table with some padding will suffice. You will need some cream or lotion for the stroking massage technique. You might want some pressure “tools” to save your fingers and hands.

[Page 27 | Healing Yourself through Self-Help Techniques | Self-Treatment]
Which direction should the pressure/force be applied? It is desirable to apply steady, deep, direct pressure to the nodule or pea-like trigger point. I have tried to represent this by the idea of a hot zone (Figure 1.4). The heart of the trigger point is located somewhere in this zone. You want to find the direction of pressure that, where possible, exactly reproduces the pain. It often amazes me that a slight change in the direction of the pressure can cause a totally different pain elsewhere. You will feel when you are “there.”

[Page 28 | Healing Yourself through Self-Help Techniques | Self-Treatment]
What creams or lotions can I use? In general, it is better to avoid oils, as they may cause you to slide off from the pressure points once you have found them. I use plain blue Nivea Creme. Alternatively, arnica cream or plain aqueous cream mixed with some vitamin E oil (with a wooden spoon) may be sufficient. Petroleum gel, talcum powder, or massage oil may also be used if you have a lanolin allergy. What is the frequency of treatment? In my experience, for self-help hands-on treatments you should perform these sessions gently no more than once a day and preferably three to four days apart. Balls, rollers, or hooks may be used for up to 10 minutes per session and up to six times a day.

Muscle spindles are made up of spiral threads, called intrafusal fibers, and nerve endings, both encased within a connective tissue sheath; together, they monitor the speed at which a muscle is lengthening. If a muscle is lengthening at speed, signals from the intrafusal fibers will fire information via the spinal cord to the nervous system, so that a nerve impulse is sent back, causing the lengthening muscle to contract. The signals provide continuous information to and from the muscle about position and power (proprioception).

[Page 49 | Skeletal Muscle, Muscle Mechanics, and Flexibility | Skeletal Muscle]
Furthermore, when a muscle is lengthened and held, it will maintain a contractile response as long as the muscle remains stretched: this facility is known as the stretch reflex arc. Muscle spindles will remain stimulated as long as the stretch is held. The classic clinical example of the stretch reflex is the knee jerk test, which involves activation of the stretch receptors in the tendon, which in turn causes a reflex contraction of the attached muscle, i.e. the quadriceps. Whereas the muscle spindles monitor the length of a muscle, the GTOs in the muscle tendon are so sensitive to tension in the muscle–tendon complex that they can respond to the contraction of a single muscle fiber. [Page 50 | Skeletal Muscle, Muscle Mechanics, and Flexibility | The Physiology of Muscle Contraction]
Muscle Fiber Shortening So how does a muscle fiber shorten? This has been explained best by the sliding filament theory (Huxley & Niedergerke 1954), which proposed that muscle fibers receive a nerve impulse (see above) that results in the release of calcium ions stored in the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR). For muscles to work effectively, energy is required, and this is created by the breakdown of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). This energy allows the calcium ions to bind with the actin and myosin filaments to form a magnetic bond, which causes the fibers to shorten, resulting in the contraction. Muscle action continues until the calcium is depleted, at which point calcium is pumped back into the SR, where it is stored until another nerve impulse arrives.

[Page 66 | Beyond the Trigger Point | Beyond the Trigger Point]
In myofascial dysfunction, “switch-off” mechanisms work to avoid the noxious stimuli. We are forced to recruit synergists, fixators, and agonists, often in a less efficient manner, to perform our daily tasks. This is fine in the short term, but over time it can lead to neuroplastic changes in the spinal cord and brain (sensitization). These mechanisms often include reflexes maintained locally in the spinal cord, and centrally in the brain. Pain Is a Big Stimulus. With regard to myofascial trigger points, the stressor is acute or chronic pain, either in a joint or in the myofascial matrix. In both cases the body “switches off” around the stimulus; this switching is maintained both locally and centrally. The phenomenon is observable in muscles around a fracture site, a slipped disc, or, for example, a frozen shoulder. Painful stimuli are often mediated by inflammation and its noxious exudates, which is part of a well-demonstrated cascade. When our feedback mechanisms are altered, the brain is forced to adapt and compensate. Pain is a highly motivating symptom for the nervous system: it is our alarm bell that something is wrong. It is important therefore to see trigger points in context: What is the body trying to achieve? Why has its tolerance/compensation broken down? Where and what is the central or core issue? I encourage my students to think like detectives: find the “tissues that are causing the symptoms” and then reflect and observe how the body has adapted over time to compensate. This requires a holistic view of the patient’s body, organs, bones, and supporting tissues, as well as their posture, nutrition, occupation, psychological state, and general wellbeing.

Chaos theory and its offshoot complexity theory represent a new paradigm in scientific thought. Complexity theory is pan-disciplinary, relating equally to economics, medicine, anthropology, history, politics, computer science, etc. It offers a framework for addressing many old and fundamental philosophical questions arising from complex systems. They are there because they have to be there; they emerge from the complexity and, under certain physiological circumstances and environments, “switch on” as trigger points. They are organizational and also part of the nervous system’s negative feedback response to noxious stimuli. Certain trigger points seem to be constantly “switched on”: I suggest that these trigger points are the strange attractors and have named them super trigger points (STPs).

[Page 70 | Beyond the Trigger Point | Myofascial Meridians]
Muscles do not operate in isolation, but might be regarded as the contractile elements within a myofascial continuum, which runs throughout the body. These meridian maps may help to explain how and why the development of primary, central trigger points in one area of the body may lead to secondary or satellite trigger points distally. The maps may also explain the cross patterns discussed in Chapter 3. The term meridian derives from acupuncture and Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM): it describes bioenergetic lines or channels that are said to flow throughout the body. Myokinetic Chains and Sublinks. The brain/body employs a range of neuromuscular strategies to coordinate muscular contraction and thus facilitate stability and spatial orientation. All of our body systems and structures work together in interdependent and connected ways. Myers (2001, 2013) presented several ideas for the myofascial component of these connections, labeling them “myofascial meridians.” Sharkey (2008) developed this concept further: he presented these meridians as a series of “functional kinetic chains.” Sharkey suggested that the body dissipates kinetic forces (energy) through the “spiral/ oblique chain, lateral chain, posterior sagittal chain, and anterior sagittal chain.” Several other secondary chains and/or connections also coexist, being both deep and superficial. Spiral Oblique Chain. The spiral oblique chain (SOC) includes the obliques (contralateral), adductors, ITB, tibialis anterior, and fibularis longus/brevis. The SOC may also include the following links: serratus anterior, ipsilateral rhomboids, and contralateral splenius capitis.

[Page 71 | Beyond the Trigger Point | Myokinetic Chains and Sublinks]
A deep PSC involves local, deep, segmentally related muscles providing localized support for motion in segments or joints (tonic or type II fibers). The PSC includes the occipitofrontalis, erector spinae, thoracolumbar fascia, multifidus, sacrotuberous ligament, and biceps femoris (short head). This link can be continued to include the gastrocnemius and plantar fascia. Figure: 6.9. The posterior sagittal chain. Posterior Oblique Chain The posterior oblique chain (POC) includes the latissimus dorsi, contralateral gluteus maximus, and thoracolumbar fascia. This chain can be continued to include the following links: ITB, tibialis anterior, and fibulares. A superficial POC involves prime movers or more global muscles that are, as the name implies, predominantly superficial. These muscles are primarily phasic and are heavily populated with type I fibers with a high resistance to fatigue. Figure: 6.10. The posterior oblique chain (POC). Anterior Sagittal Chain The anterior sagittal chain (ASC) includes the dorsal surface of the foot, tibial periosteum, rectus femoris (including articularis genu), anterior inferior iliac spine (AIIS), pubic tubercle, rectus abdominis, sternal periosteum, SCM, and periosteum of the mastoid process. The Niel-Asher Technique® (NAT) The NAT is an advanced trigger point technique, utilizing the neurophysiology behind myofascial trigger points in a novel and deliberate way. The technique uses deep stroking massage and compression/inhibition techniques blended together in a choreographed algorithm. In NAT, trigger points are regarded not as the familiar knots of muscular dysfunction, but as “inputs” to the spinal cord and CNS. This is because trigger points are exquisitely painful and, as discussed above, intentionally stimulating mechanoreceptors embedded within and around the trigger points (and joints) generates a novel “neural signature,” which affects the spinal cord and the somatic cortices.

[Page 73 | Beyond the Trigger Point | NAT Theory]
Many thousands of frozen shoulders have now been successfully treated with NAT. It is evidence based and is now used to treat a wide range of musculoskeletal conditions. NAT Theory: Intentionally stimulating mechanoreceptors embedded within and around the trigger points (and joints) generates a novel “neural signature,” which affects the spinal cord and the somatic cortices. NAT deliberately utilizes some of the automatic reflexes associated with trigger points, including: Co-coordination, Reciprocal inhibition, Post-isometric relaxation, Post-activation depression, Pure facilitation, Co-facilitation, Autonomic responses, “Pain gate”, Spinal cord reflex responses, Neuroplasticity. The nervous system responds to these input sequences by releasing the “holding pattern,” normalizing motor unit output, and improving co-coordination. Clinically, after each NAT session, patients describe a sense of joints being “oiled inside” or feeling that “normal” muscular control has been regained. With regard to somatic dysfunction, NAT is readily used to reinvigorate and release protective joint postures (such as with spondylolisthesis) and/or treat protective spasm around joint problems (such as an arthritic hip).

[Page 74 | Beyond the Trigger Point | 3-D NAT Sequences and Regional Trigger Points]
Furthermore, NAT sequences seem to tone or re-activate the muscles around the joint; as an automatic response, there is also an increase in strength and power. This is one of the reasons why NAT was successfully used by physical therapists for members of the Canadian and Australian teams in the 2012 London Olympics. Evidence for this phenomenon has been supported by research at Addenbrooke’s Hospital in Cambridge, UK (Weis et al. 2003). Patents with long-standing shoulder pain and weakness treated with NAT demonstrated a significant improvement in active ROM (P<0.002) and in strength and power (P<0.046) over and above standard physical therapy and a hands-on placebo, even though no exercises were given to the NAT group. 3-D NAT Sequences: I have included some of the 3-D NAT release techniques as examples at the end of each of the colored muscle sections (Chapters 7–12). Here is an example of a basic NAT treatment for mild to moderate osteoarthritic hip (O/A hip): 1. Assess ROM. 2. Examine the muscles and note trigger points. 3. Treat trigger points in the adductors (with patient in a side-lying position on the affected side), using deep stroking massage in one direction only, from the knee to the groin. Figure: 6.13. Supine inhibition of the pectineus using the elbow. Altered Antagonism: Having treated well over a thousand frozen shoulders, I have observed that in someone with acute shoulder pain the biceps brachii and triceps brachii pairing stops operating properly. Instead, the biceps brachii and the infraspinatus pair off; similarly, the triceps brachii and the pectoralis minor seem to change their functional relationship. NAT takes these functional antagonistic changes into account during treatment sequences. Attractors Within Complex Human Systems. The human body as a complex dynamic system can be seen to exhibit a range of attractors operating at various levels, and operating in the parts and in the whole. The following systems seem to demonstrate this principle in some way (this list is indicative only): The heart, with point, periodic, and strange attractors operating (Mills 2005). Homeostatic (dynamic) functions, such as body temperature or the menstrual cycle. Spindle formation in embryology. The liver—macro and micro functions. The reticulo-lymphatic system. Osteogenesis and fascial dissemination.

[Page 92 | Therapeutic Technique Protocols | Super Trigger Points (STPs)]
I would like to go further and assert that certain PMRs are class 3 attractors—or strange attractors—within the complex myofascial web; they emerge and are activated on demand. They are there because they have to be there; they emerge from the complexity and, under certain physiological circumstances and environments, “switch on” as trigger points. They are organizational and also part of the nervous system’s negative feedback response to noxious stimuli. Certain trigger points seem to be constantly “switched on”: I suggest that these trigger points are the strange attractors and have named them super trigger points (STPs).

[Page 93 | Therapeutic Technique Protocols | Super Trigger Points (STPs)]
Super Trigger Points (STPs). STPs seem to be active all the time in everyone: it is like they “have to be there.” They are the myofascial strange attractors. Releasing trigger points in these muscles appears to have greater systemic effects than expected, often including profound physiological effects (such as autonomic changes). These effects are well beyond the “normal” trigger point reactions, hence the name “super” trigger points. I have observed that incorporating these “super” trigger points into a treatment protocol acts as a type of shortcut, rapidly releasing deep-seated and chronic pain syndromes. At the end of each muscle chapter, I have suggested some basic NAT protocols which incorporate STPs. Examples of these physiological or “super” trigger points can be found in: Figure: 6.6. Connections: Infraspinatus, pectorals, teres minor, latissimus dorsi, triceps brachii, posterior deltoid, and supraspinatus . Reciprocal Inhibition (RI) Technique (Indications: acute settings): 1. Identify the host muscle and take it into relaxation. 2. Ask the patient to contract the antagonist muscle against 35–45% isometric resistance .

[Page 166 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Teres Major]
Name Derivation: Latin 'teres' (rounded, finely shaped) and 'major' (larger) . Origin: Oval area on the lower third of the posterior surface of the inferior angle of the scapula. Insertion: Medial lip of the intertubercular sulcus on the anterior surface of the humerus. Nerve: Lower subscapular nerve C5–7 . Action: Medial rotation and extension of the arm at the glenohumeral joint. Basic Functional Movement: Reaching into the back pocket . Referred Pain Patterns: Deep pain into the posterior glenohumeral joint and an oval zone (5–10 cm) of pain in the posterior deltoid area, which can radiate strongly to the long head of the biceps brachii. Diffuse pain also extends into the dorsum of the forearm .

[Page 167 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Teres Major (Continued)]
Practitioner Hands-On Techniques: Indicated methods include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, muscle energy, and positional release; wet needling is also mentioned . Advice: Use heat or warmth (hot showers), avoid heavy steering wheels, monitor gym activities, hug a pillow at night, and perform plenty of self-stretching . Indications: Frozen shoulder syndrome, pain reaching above the head, slight pain at rest, pain when driving, and impingement syndromes (sometimes misdiagnosed as thoracic outlet syndrome) . Self-Help Technique: Includes studying anatomy and using a pressure ball on the back of the head/trigger point for up to 10 minutes until pain eases .

[Page 168 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Teres Major (Causes and Connections)]
Causes: Sports-related forcefully overhead lifting, post-shoulder fracture/dislocation, frozen shoulder syndrome, sudden unexpected loading (e.g., a fall), and prolonged immobility in a sling . Differential Diagnosis: Impingement syndromes, rotator cuff tendinopathy, cervical neuropatterns (C6–C7), thoracic outlet syndrome, and supraspinatus calcification . Connections: Rhomboids, triceps brachii (long head), latissimus dorsi, teres minor, pectorals, posterior deltoid, C6 or C7 neck disc issues, and subdeltoid bursitis .

[Page 169 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Biceps Brachii]
Origin: Long head: supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula; Short head: tip of the coracoid process . Insertion: Radial tuberosity. Nerve: Musculocutaneous nerve C5, 6. Action: Powerful flexor of the forearm at the elbow and supinates the forearm (described as putting in a corkscrew and pulling out the cork). It is an accessory flexor of the arm at the glenohumeral joint . Basic Functional Movement: Picking up an object or bringing food to the mouth. Referred Pain Patterns: Localized pain with an intense ellipse superficially located over the long head tendon; also refers pain into the anterior cubital fossa .

[Page 170 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Shoulder Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)]
A detailed Step-by-Step NAT protocol for shoulder pain. STEP 1: Study anatomy and fiber direction of supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor/major, pectoralis minor, serratus anterior, subscapularis, coracobrachialis, deltoid, pectoralis major, and subclavius . STEP 2: Sitting ICT to supraspinatus and upper trapezius. STEP 3: Massage area generously. STEP 4: Side-lying ICT to deltoid (stroking upward only) and teres minor (letting hand fall off the bed). STEP 5: Supine ICT to pectoralis minor and infraspinatus (a Super Trigger Point). This step can be very painful, so deep breathing is advised .

[Page 174 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Rhomboids Overview]
Treatment Protocol: Steps 4-6 involve applying sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, meeting the next barrier, and changing the direction of pressure for better results. Name Derivation: Greek 'rhomboeides' (parallelogram shaped). Description: Rhomboid major is often continuous with rhomboid minor; they are named for their shape.

[Page 175 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Rhomboid Details]
Origin: Minor: spinous processes of C7 and T1, lower ligamentum nuchae; Major: spinous processes of T2–5. Insertion: Minor: medial border of scapula at the spine; Major: medial border of scapula from the spine to the inferior angle. Nerve: Dorsal scapular nerve C4, 5. Action: Elevates and retracts the scapula.

[Page 176 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Serratus Anterior]
Origin: Lateral surfaces of upper eight or nine ribs. Insertion: Anterior surface of medial border of scapula. Nerve: Long thoracic nerve C5–7. Action: Rotates scapula for abduction/flexion; protracts scapula and holds it to the chest wall. Basic Functional Movement: Reaching forward for something barely in reach or pushing a door open.

[Page 177 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Pectoralis Minor]
Insertion: Coracoid process of scapula. Nerve: Medial pectoral nerve C5–T1. Action: Draws shoulder downward; protracts scapula; acts as an accessory muscle of inspiration. Basic Functional Movement: Pushing on the arms of a chair to stand up. Referred Pain Patterns: Chest flaring in muscle fiber direction; radiations into the medial upper extremity to the medial two and a half fingers.

[Page 178 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Latissimus Dorsi]
Treatment Note: Step 6 involves repeating the stroking in the opposite direction. Name Derivation: Latin 'latissimus' (widest) and 'dorsi' (of the back). Origin: Spinous processes of lower six thoracic vertebrae, lumbar vertebrae (via thoracolumbar fascia), iliac crest, and lower ribs. Insertion: Floor of intertubercular sulcus of humerus.

[Page 179 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Supraspinatus]
Treatment Note: Step 6 involves changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'supra' (above) and 'spina' (spine). Description: A member of the rotator cuff. Origin: Medial two-thirds of supraspinous fossa of scapula. Insertion: Superior facet on the greater tubercle of humerus. Nerve: Suprascapular nerve C5, 6. Action: Initiates abduction of the arm to 15 degrees.

[Page 180 | Muscles of the Shoulder and Upper Arm | Teres Minor]
Treatment Note: Step 6 involves changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'teres' (rounded) and 'minor' (smaller). Description: A member of the rotator cuff. Origin: Upper two-thirds of the posterior surface of the scapula adjacent to the lateral border. Insertion: Inferior facet on greater tubercle of humerus. Nerve: Axillary nerve C5, 6. Action: Lateral rotation of the arm at the glenohumeral joint.

[Page 181 | Muscles of the Forearm and Hand | Supinator]
Treatment Note: Steps 6-8 involve isometric contraction held for 15–30 seconds, repeated 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'supinus' (lying on the back). Description: Supinator is almost entirely concealed by superficial muscles. Origin: Superficial part: lateral epicondyle of humerus, radial collateral and anular ligaments; Deep part: supinator crest of ulna. Insertion: Lateral surface of radius superior to the anterior oblique line. Nerve: Posterior interosseous nerve C5, 6, (7).

[Page 182 | Muscles of the Forearm and Hand | Opponens Pollicis]
Treatment Note: Steps 5-6 involve gently lengthening the muscle during relaxation and repeating usually 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'opponens' (opposing) and 'pollicis' (of the thumb). Description: Usually partly fused with flexor pollicis brevis and deep to abductor pollicis brevis. Origin: Flexor retinaculum and tubercle of trapezium. Insertion: Entire length of radial border of first metacarpal. Nerve: Recurrent branch of median nerve C8, T1.

[Page 185 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Hip and Thigh Self-Help]
Self-Help Technique: 1. Observe anatomy. 2. Run downward from the rim of the pelvis toward the front of the thigh, identifying painful spots. 3. Work using thumbs with small scooping movements. 4. Pause on knots until pain subsides; pressure may be increased. 5. Follow the stroke to the end. Indications: Pain in low back/buttocks, night pain, pain on side lying, post-hip/spinal surgery, sitting on a wallet, leg length discrepancy, arthritic hip, and pregnancy.

[Page 186 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Piriformis]
Treatment Note: Steps 6-8 involve isometric contraction held for 15–30 seconds, repeated 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'pirum' (pear) and 'forma' (shape). Description: Leaves the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen; along with obturator internus, it is a muscle of the pelvic wall. Origin: Anterior surface of sacrum between anterior sacral foramina. Insertion: Medial side of superior border of greater trochanter. Nerve: Branches from sacral nerves S1, 2. Action: Laterally rotates extended femur; abducts flexed femur; holds femur head in acetabulum.

[Page 187 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Obturator Internus and Short Lateral Rotators]
Treatment Note: Steps 4-6 involve relaxing the muscle, gently lengthening it, and repeating usually 3 times. Name Derivation: Latin 'obturare' (to obstruct) and 'internus' (internal). Description: The group includes Gemellus superior/inferior, obturator internus/externus, and quadratus femoris. These muscles work together kinetically, starting at the top of the femur and inserting on the posterior femur and greater trochanter. Pain is felt locally at the greater trochanter, over the buttock into the groin, and in the posterior thigh.

[Page 188 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Pectineus]
Treatment Note: Step 6 involves repeating the stroking in the opposite direction. Name Derivation: Latin 'pecten' (comb) and 'pectinatus' (comb shaped). Description: Sandwiched between psoas major and adductor longus. Origin: Pecten pubis and adjacent pelvic bone. Insertion: Oblique line from base of lesser trochanter to linea aspera of femur. Nerve: Femoral nerve L2, 3. Action: Adducts and flexes thigh. Basic Functional Movement: Walking in a straight line. Referred Pain Patterns: Strong 8–12 cm zone in anterior groin with diffuse radiations toward the anteromedial thigh.

[Page 189 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Sartorius]
Name Derivation: Latin 'sartor' (tailor). Description: The most superficial muscle of the anterior thigh and the longest strap muscle in the body. Its medial border forms the lateral boundary of the femoral triangle. Action: Puts the lower limbs in the seated cross-legged position of a tailor. Origin: Anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).

[Page 190 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Quadriceps (Rectus Femoris and Vastus Lateralis)]
Treatment Note: Steps 5-6 involve increasing pressure on trigger points and changing the direction of pressure. Name Derivation: Latin 'rectus' (straight), 'femoris' (of the thigh), 'vastus' (vast), and 'lateralis' (relating to the side). Description: The four quadriceps are rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius. All cross the knee joint. Rectus femoris has two origins (reflected and straight heads), the latter unique to humans due to upright posture.

[Page 191 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Hamstrings Overview]
The differential diagnosis for hamstrings includes sciatica, radiculopathy, muscle tears, osteitis, bursitic osteoarthritis of the knee, knee joint dysfunction, and tenosynovitis . It is connected to the piriformis, popliteus, gluteals, obturator internus, vastus lateralis, plantaris, gastrocnemius, and thoracolumbar paraspinal muscles . The page outlines the Contract-Relax-Antagonist-Contract (CRAC) technique as a combined neuromodulatory input of post-isometric relaxation and reciprocal inhibition, excellent for chronic conditions [1, 2].

[Page 192 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Adductors]
Includes the adductor brevis, longus, and magnus . Basic Functional Movement: Bringing the second leg in or out of a car . Referred Pain Patterns: (1) two zones localized around the anterior hip and above the knee (5–8 cm); (2) the whole anteromedial thigh from the inguinal ligament to the medial knee joint; (3) the medial thigh from the hip to the knee . High adductor magnus trigger points can also refer pain to the pelvic region .

[Page 193 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Adductors Overview]
The (Inhibition) Compression Technique is detailed for active central trigger points [5, 6]. The practitioner applies gradually increasing pressure to the trigger point while lengthening the host muscle until a palpable barrier is hit, experienced as discomfort but not pain . Pressure is sustained until the point softens and is then repeated to reach the next barrier .

[Page 194 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Pectineus]
Name Derivation: Latin 'pecten' (comb) and 'pectinatus' (comb shaped) . Description: Sandwiched between the psoas major and adductor longus . Origin: Pecten pubis and adjacent bone of pelvis . Insertion: Oblique line from the base of the lesser trochanter to the linea aspera of the femur . Nerve: Femoral nerve L2, 3 . Action: Adducts and flexes the thigh at the hip joint . Basic Functional Movement: Walking along a straight line . Referred Pain Patterns: Strong 8–12 cm zone of pain in the anterior groin with diffuse radiations in an oval toward the anteromedial thigh .

[Page 195 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Pectineus Overview]
Treatment includes the Deep Stroking Massage Technique. Step 5: Apply sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, continuing the stroke toward the attachment of the taut band . Step 6: Repeat the stroking in the opposite direction . Illustrations also show the supine inhibition of the pectineus using the elbow for a stronger lever .

[Page 196 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Sartorius]
Description: The most superficial muscle of the anterior thigh and the longest strap muscle in the body; its medial border forms the lateral boundary of the femoral triangle . Action: Puts the lower limbs in the seated cross-legged position . Origin: Anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) . Connections include the vastus medialis, biceps femoris, gracilis, pectineus, and tensor fasciae latae .

[Page 197 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Sartorius Overview]
Utilizes the (Inhibition) Compression Technique. Step 4: Apply sustained pressure until the trigger point softens, which can take from a few seconds to several minutes . Self-help advice includes reviewing anatomy, identifying the trigger point, pausing on it until it softens, and massaging the area afterwards [10, 11].

[Page 198 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Quadriceps]
Description: Comprises the rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius; all four cross the knee joint . Rectus femoris has two origins (reflected and straight heads), the latter being unique to humans due to our upright posture . Trigger points here are known for causing a 'buckling knee' sensation .

[Page 199 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Quadriceps Overview]
Treatment notes emphasize increasing pressure on trigger points and changing the direction of pressure for better outcomes . Methods indicated include spray and stretch, dry needling, deep stroking massage, compression, and positional release . Self-stretching is advised thereafter three times per day .

[Page 201 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Regional Trigger Points for Hip, Thigh, and Knee Pain]
This regional index lists muscles associated with pain in the hip, thigh, and knee, along with their page references: Adductor brevis (192), Adductor longus (192), Adductor magnus (192), Biceps femoris (190), Fibularis longus (208), Flexor digitorum brevis (222), Gastrocnemius (210), Gluteus maximus (178), Gluteus medius (182), Gluteus minimus (184), Iliocostalis lumborum (102), Iliocostalis thoracis (102), Iliopsoas (120), Obturator internus (188), Pectineus (194), Piriformis (186), Popliteus (216), Quadratus lumborum (118), Rectus abdominis (116), Rectus femoris (198), Sartorius (196), Semimembranosus (190), Semitendinosus (190), Soleus (214), Tensor fasciae latae (180), Vastus lateralis (198), and Vastus medialis (198).

[Page 202 | Muscles of the Hip and Thigh | Knee Pain Treatment Sequence (NAT)]
The step-by-step NAT protocol for knee pain includes: STEP 1: Study the anatomy and fiber direction of the quadriceps (vastus lateralis, rectus femoris, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis) and popliteus. STEP 2: Prone ICT (Inhibition Compression Technique) to the popliteus. STEP 3: Deep stroking massage to the area generously, moving upward only. STEP 4: Perform supine medial and lateral gapping articulation to the knee joint with slight flexion; followed by ICT to the vastus medialis/lateralis insertions, the quadriceps expansion (above the patella), and the ligamentum patellae (a Super Trigger Point) just below the patella.

[Page 203 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Regional Trigger Points for Leg, Ankle, and Foot Pain]
This index provides references for muscles related to lower leg and foot pain: Abductor digiti minimi (222), Abductor hallucis (222), Adductor brevis (192), Adductor hallucis (224), Adductor longus (192), Extensor digitorum brevis (222), Extensor digitorum longus (206), Extensor hallucis longus (206), Fibulares (208), Flexor digitorum brevis (222), Flexor digitorum longus (218), Flexor hallucis brevis (224), Gastrocnemius (210), Gluteus minimus (182), Interossei (224), Plantaris (212), Popliteus (216), Quadratus plantae (224), Semimembranosus (190), Semitendinosus (190), Soleus (214), Tibialis anterior (204), Tibialis posterior (220), and Vastus lateralis (198).

[Page 204 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Tibialis Anterior]
Origin: Lateral surface of the tibia and the adjacent interosseous membrane. Insertion: Medial and inferior surfaces of the medial cuneiform and adjacent surfaces on the base of the first metatarsal. Nerve: Deep fibular nerve L4, 5. Action: Dorsiflexes the foot at the ankle joint and inverts the foot; providing dynamic support to the medial arch. Basic Functional Movement: Facilitates walking and running by preventing the foot from slapping the ground after heel strike and lifting the foot clear during the swing phase.

[Page 206 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Extensor Digitorum Longus and Extensor Hallucis Longus]
Description: Like the hand, the EDL forms extensor hoods on the dorsum of the proximal phalanges, joined by lumbrical tendons and the extensor digitorum brevis. Origin (EDL): Proximal half of the medial surface of the fibula and lateral tibial condyle. Origin (EHL): Middle half of the medial surface of the fibula and the interosseous membrane. Insertion (EDL): Along the dorsal surface of the four lateral toes, attaching to the bases of middle and distal phalanges. Insertion (EHL): Base of the distal phalanx of the great toe. Nerve: Deep fibular nerve L5, S1. Action (EDL): Extends the lateral four toes and dorsiflexes the foot. Action (EHL): Extends the great toe and dorsiflexes the foot.

[Page 208 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Fibulares]
Name Derivation: Latin fibula (pin/buckle); comprising fibularis longus, brevis, and tertius. Description: The insertion of the fibularis longus helps maintain the transverse and lateral longitudinal arches of the foot. A slip from the fibularis brevis often joins the EDL tendon for the little toe, sometimes called the peroneus digiti minimi.

[Page 209 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Fibulares Overview and Treatment]
Connections: Tensor fasciae latae (TFL), gluteus minimus, extensor digitorum longus/brevis, and extensor hallucis brevis. Treatment Protocol: Utilizes the Post-Isometric (PIR) Technique (Steps 1–6) as described for the extensors, focusing on subacute to chronic settings. Step 6 involves repeating the lengthening phase three times.

[Page 210 | Muscles of the Leg and Foot | Gastrocnemius]
Name Derivation: Greek gaster (stomach) and kneme (lower leg). Origin: Medial head arises from the posterior surface of the distal femur just superior to the medial condyle; Lateral head arises from the upper postero-lateral surface of the lateral femoral condyle. Insertion: Posterior surface of the calcaneus via the Achilles (calcaneal) tendon.
